terms.definition: True positives are correctly predicted positives. False positives are negative cases predicted as positive. False negatives are positive cases the model missed.
Of the items predicted positive, the fraction that were truly positive. Useful when false positives are expensive.
Of the truly positive items, the fraction the model found. Useful when misses are expensive.
The harmonic mean of precision and recall. It rewards a balance between finding positives and avoiding false alarms.
F1 computed independently for each class, then averaged equally. Good when small classes matter.
F1 computed from globally pooled decisions. In single-label multi-class tasks it often behaves like accuracy.
Per-class F1 averaged by class support. It reflects dataset prevalence but can hide poor small-class performance.
The area under the true-positive-rate versus false-positive-rate curve across thresholds. Useful for ranking quality, less informative under extreme imbalance than PR-AUC.
A segmentation overlap score equal to twice the intersection divided by total predicted plus true area.
Intersection over Union: overlap divided by the union of predicted and true regions.
A language-model metric derived from cross-entropy. Lower perplexity means the model assigns higher probability to the observed text.
A strict metric requiring the predicted answer to match the reference exactly, often used in QA and extraction.
A classification loss that penalizes assigning low probability to the true class. In PyTorch, CrossEntropyLoss expects raw logits.
Increasing loss contribution from underrepresented or more important classes. Weights should be tuned because extreme values can overcorrect.
A regularization technique that softens hard labels by assigning a small amount of probability mass to non-target classes.
Ignoring selected positions or examples in a loss calculation, commonly used for padded tokens or unknown labels.
When a model learns training-specific patterns that do not generalize to validation or production data.
Techniques that discourage brittle fits, such as weight decay, dropout, augmentation, label smoothing, and early stopping.
Repeatedly training and validating on different data splits to estimate generalization more robustly than a single split.
A cross-validation splitter that preserves class proportions across folds. It is not appropriate when time or group boundaries matter more.
A splitter that respects chronological order by training on earlier rows and validating on later rows.
A splitter that keeps all rows from the same group in either training or validation, not both.
Any path where training or model selection sees information that would not be available at prediction time.
A leakage type where future information influences features, preprocessing, validation, or labels for earlier predictions.
A leakage type where related entities, such as the same user or patient, appear in both training and validation.
A difference between the data distribution used for training and the distribution encountered in production.
A change in feature distribution while the relationship between features and labels is assumed mostly stable.
A change in the relationship between features and labels, such as user behavior changing after a product update.
A repeating pattern tied to calendar or cycle structure, such as hourly, weekly, or yearly effects.
A previous value used as a feature, such as sales from seven days ago.
Correlation between a time series and shifted versions of itself.
Predicting one step ahead, then feeding that prediction back in to predict later steps.
Training separate outputs or models for each forecast horizon instead of recursively feeding predictions back in.
A unit of text processed by a model, such as a word piece, character span, or punctuation mark.
The language-modeling objective of predicting the next token given previous context.
Continuing training from a pretrained model on task-specific data or instructions.
The greatest integer less than or equal to a value.
The largest value among inputs, often used for clipping, thresholds, or reductions.
A relationship that moves in only one direction: as one value increases, the other never decreases, or vice versa.
